text: The video shows a protruding belly button, indicative of an umbilical hernia, in preparation for ventral hernia repair with mesh.
Inside the abdomen, a hole in the abdominal wall is visible. The surgeon pulls the peritoneum layer down and away from the fascia, the strongest layer of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneum, revealing a yellow layer of adipose tissue in the area.
The surgeon begins removing the peritoneum from the fascia, preparing to situate the mesh directly onto the strong fascia layer of the abdominal wall.

The surgeon encounters the hernia, revealing a significant amount of yellow adipose tissue protruding through the hernia space. As the tissue is extracted, the fascia becomes visible and skeletonized.
The surgeon uses a left-handed instrument to measure the true edges of the hernia defect, estimating its diameter to be approximately three centimeters.

The surgeon selects an 11.4 cm circular mesh for the hernia repair. The surgical field shows the continued separation of the peritoneum from the fascia, extending beyond the hernia defect to create a pocket large enough to accommodate the mesh.

The surgeon assesses the exposed fascia to determine if it is sufficient for mesh placement, then proceeds to close the hernia defect using sutures. The closure of the defect is visually observed, which is known to contribute to post-operative pain.

The surgeon tightens the sutures under tension to bring the edges of the hernia defect together. Once closed, the mesh is positioned to lay flat against the abdominal wall, maximizing its surface area to distribute the tension.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it overlaps the edges adequately to bind securely to the abdominal wall in all directions.

The surgeon has separated the flimsy peritoneum from the stronger fascia, allowing the mesh to bind directly to the fascia for a robust repair. The mesh is sutured to the abdominal wall using absorbable stitches, which may cause temporary pain near the belly button.

The surgeon positions the mesh correctly and proceeds to close the pocket created in the peritoneum. The peritoneum layer is used to conceal the mesh from the intestines, preventing scar tissue formation that could lead to small bowel obstructions.

The recovery is very important in this operation. Until the body heals and scars to the mesh, any of these sutures can be ruptured and the repair can come undone. The goal is to fix the hernia and leave the abdomen as close to the way we found it as possible. With the mesh placed within the walls of the abdomen, the risk of complications is significantly minimized.

Years after I had performed the same repair on another patient, I returned to his abdomen to remove his gallbladder. The inside of his abdomen had no adhesions to the intestines and no exposed mesh. You can barely make out the edges of the perimeter of the mesh. There are no incisions at the belly button. The three separate eight millimeter incisions are on the left of the abdomen shown here. I hope this video was helpful.
